title: P3II Instrumentality A Reinterpretation of Past Occurences
display_title: P3II Instrumentality A Reinterpretation of Past Occurences
wikitext:
[]: P3II Instrumentality-- A Reinterpretation of Past Occurences             Quote Post by ran1 » Tue Jun 15, 2010 8:18 pm What I'm proposing will probably be my most controversial theory in awhile. In reality, it's not even really my theory that came 100% from my own observations-- it's a mix of my observations, other astute cineastes like Xard and Jimbo, Tines, Reichu, Shin-Sieki, among others, who have succeeded in picking apart little bits and pieces of P3II. This is basically using what I've learned from them and then applying that, some sequence theory and a little montage theory into quite possibly the biggest fanwank in history. Get ready, my nigs. First and foremost, I'll be dealing with those scenes that make up the sequence that occurs just following Shinji's acceptance of instrumentality and just prior to the Hell Train scene. I'm under the impression that unearthing this material will prove essential in developing a complete understanding of the Hell Train scene, because it is important to determine exactly what the hell train scene actually is. ==== Now, onto the analysis. For the sake of concise argumentation I've decided to divide the following montages and scenes in the sequence. They will be referred to thusly: Scene 1: The Sandbox Scene 2: Shinji/Asuka Intercourse Scene 3: Misato/Kaji Intercourse Scene 4: Kiss Scene Scene 5: Montage Sequence {Scene 6: Hell Train* Scene 7: Is Shinji Ikari Gonna have to Choke a Bitch?*} *Expect corollaries, this first post will only deal with Scenes 1-6 in great detail. I want to be able to have some feedback on whether or not I am being clear enough here before I move onto the big stuff. Now for the thesis of this piece. I'd leave it in the essay somewhere, but I want to make it as clear as day now: The following scenes all occurred at a given point in the NGE time-line. Some of these scenes were implied, some of them were actually shown in the series, and some of them were not mentioned at all but were likely to have happened and are used for their metaphoric qualities. Scene 1: The Sandbox Sequence [wkimg width=300]File:EoE_Sandbox_child1.jpg[/wkimg] [wkimg width=300]File:EoE_Sandbox_child2.jpg[/wkimg][wkimg width=300]File:EoE Sandbox mother.jpg[/wkimg] Episode 26' wrote: Shinji (voice): I see... It's just like when I started the cello. I thought that if I came here, I could find something... Child A: Come on, Shinji! Child B: Let's build a castle! Child-Shinji (smiling): Okay. Child A: Oh, here comes Mama! Child B: We have to go home now! Child A: Bye-bye! Child A and B (laughing): Mama... Mama! (Shinji builds a pyramid (NERV headquarters) alone, and then destroys it... Crying, he starts to build again)

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Tue Jun 15, 2010 8:38 pm While an event like the sandbox scene MIGHT have happened to Shinji as a child on some level, Pre-3rd Impact Pre-Instrumentality is an otherwise metaphysical event. Quantum/Bookend Lilith who also appears in the hell train sequences reinforces metaphysics can happen and are a time-less, space-less event. The script designates Misato IS the mother in the sandbox scene . Also, you're a few years too late to the Shinji/Asuka=Sex overanalysis . Interesting theories to read and discuss, but it is hard to take these as "fact", just IMO. I'd contribute more, but anything I really have to say on these subjects were already mentioned in previous threads.

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Tue Jun 15, 2010 9:25 pm Re the sandbox scene, I think one of the unused scripts has a note asking "Should this be young Asuka?" with respect to the doll children. Not that that's necessarily relevant to the final film.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Tue Jun 15, 2010 9:30 pm The one thing that easily solves the question for me is to remember the hospital scene in EoE. It is robbed of its power -- not to mention sense -- if Shinji and Asuka had already had sex. His reaction is that of someone who has just seen naked boob in the flesh for the first time. Followed by acting upon his first, er, 'comfort instinct' that comes to mind.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Tue Jun 15, 2010 9:37 pm Monk Ed wrote: The one thing that easily solves the question for me is to remember the hospital scene in EoE. It is robbed of its power -- not to mention sense -- if Shinji and Asuka had already had sex. His reaction is that of someone who has just seen naked boob in the flesh for the first time. Followed by acting upon his first, er, 'comfort instinct' that comes to mind.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Tue Jun 15, 2010 9:41 pm @self. Okay, not the first time seeing naked boob. (Hey, most of those don't even feature nipple!) But his reaction to Asuka's still strikes me as something that makes a lot less sense if he's already had sex with her before. The idea that scene presented to me is that to him, Asuka is untouchable.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Tue Jun 15, 2010 9:52 pm Yes to everything. Sorry, I don't have anything more constructive to say, but at the current time my eyeballs are aching and my head is throbbing. In particular, I see no reason to disbelieve your fanwank that Shin and Asuka humped boredly and frustratingly. In fact, putting it that way only makes it seem far more probable.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Tue Jun 15, 2010 9:53 pm @MonkEd: So you're implying that Shinji would more likely rape Asuka in her sleep in that scene or something like that as opposed to masturbation? I seriously doubt it. The otaku criticism still remains whether or not he slept with her. The masturbation itself is the crux of the critique, regardless of whether or not he popped her cherry.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Tue Jun 15, 2010 10:03 pm ran1 wrote: @MonkEd: So you're implying that Shinji would more likely rape Asuka in her sleep in that scene or something like that as opposed to masturbation? I seriously doubt it.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 15, 2010 10:41 pm I'm far from convinced. IMO, none of this really happened to Shinji as it is presented here. 1 = psychological theater, a symbolic reenactment of his life as he sees it (and foreshadowing his decision to let the world burn when he feels he's once again been abandoned) 2 = if the prior can be symbolic, why not this too? 3 = just a memory that Shinji is intruding upon. 4&5 = a current discussion with visuals put together from fragmented, out of context bits that actually happened to create something new, as well as a portmanteau of two scenes that leads to entirely new events.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Tue Jun 15, 2010 10:44 pm By the way, both Kaji/Misato sex flashbacks in TV25 and M26' are an Instrumentality reference/flashback to the week-long college sexathon mentioned by young Ritsuko in 21'. Like people have said in threads similiar to this one, the Shinji/Asuka realtionship as a whole really DOES lose the power of its fucked up-ness if the viewers are watching NGE flashbacks instead of Instrumentality-style twisted retakes of NGE events. Besides, Asuka ran off to Hikari's in 23' and a broken down house in 24'.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Tue Jun 15, 2010 10:53 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Besides, Asuka ran off to Hikari's in 23' and a broken down house in 24'.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Jun 15, 2010 11:38 pm Monk Ed wrote: Come to think of it... When was Shinji told?

[]: Re: P3II Instrumentality-- A Reinterpretation of Past Occure             Quote Post by manny_kaltz » Wed Jun 16, 2010 1:47 am ran1 wrote: What we have here is essentially the perfect introduction and summary of Shinji's problems – he attempts to build relationships (represented by the sandcastle) with others (represented by the Asuka and Rei figurines) but for one reason or another they all fall apart. But the question remains-- why do they fall apart? The answer in this scene seems to be twofold: First: It is because people leave Shinji alone and abandon him. This is acutely represented by the characters in here-- Shinji feels abandoned (and most recently, terrified) by Rei III, mostly because the new Rei kept her distance from Shinji until she came to terms with her vision of him (whereas Shinji had the opportunity to grow close to Rei II). Shinji feels abandoned by Asuka as a result of her breakdown following her mind-rape as a result of the Arael attack, though it is clear their relationship was already somewhat deteriorating prior to that point. And finally, we arrive at Misato, who has failed Shinji twice: first by putting the moves on him in Episode 23, and finally by her cold words at the end of Episode 24. Second: Shinji also plays a hand in ruining the relationships. Seeing the pyramid as a relationship (also has implications of NERV) we see Shinji destroy it with his own actions. This can be seen in the ways that Shinji will sour his relationship with Asuka when she realizes that he masturbated over her comatose body, and later, the choking scene.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Wed Jun 16, 2010 2:16 am Sachi wrote: Around this time of the series, Shinji has more or less pulled himself away from everybody, but he still seems to listen. In 21, when Misato gets home and listens to Kaji's voicemail, Shinji hears her crying, and probably heard enough to know what was going on. While Shinji can be quite dense at times, I doubt this went over his head.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Wed Jun 16, 2010 2:23 am Monk Ed wrote: Shinji doesn't have the appropriate reaction then either

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Wed Jun 16, 2010 2:36 am Legendary wrote: Shinji is listening to his music. He can hear the phone in the background, but the conversation is indistinct. I can say this with absolute certainty because that's how I hear the world when I'm listening to my music.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Jun 16, 2010 3:20 am No, not so much. In the shot from the side you can clearly see that Shinji does NOT have his headphones in, so the mysterious music should not be a problem. Maybe it's just a cast of Shinji playing dumb because he doesn't want to get involved in that line of discussion. I suspect that Shinji actually listened to the message before Misato did, hence his even more withdrawn than normal behavior when she gets home. If he didn't understand it then, her breakdown afterwards probably clarified things.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Wed Jun 16, 2010 7:04 am A quick note: After doing a little research, it turns out that the masturbation scene occurs in the first minute of the film (providing that we disallow opening credits, which in their own are not 'part' of a film's run time per say--) and the masturbation scene is the one scene that plays out in full at the 59:30 minute mark. What I see here is some delicious running-time juxtaposition. Masturbation that opens and closes the film. To elaborate, running time juxtaposition is when a director or editor, who it is isn't important, decides to juxtapose two scenes that are similar in set-up or content at important points in the film. Now before you guys go "HERP just a coincidence DERP", allow me to show you some directors who do use running time juxtaposition: -Steven Soderbergh in Che [2008] -Sion Sono in Suicide Club [1999] -Jean Luc Godard in numerous works Now I'm not going to make any further comparisons, but at least know that Anno was indirectly influenced by Godard, and that this juxtaposition encompasses two sexual acts that essentially the same thing: masturbation. In the first case, we have actual, hand on wank masturbation. In the second case, furtive, angry, loveless sex, and to paraphrase postmodern critic Slavoj Zizek: Sex without love is like masturbation with a partner.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Wed Jun 16, 2010 8:52 am I find myself in agreement with almost all of the conclusions of scene 01. This is almost certainly a repurposed memory from Shinji's past. However, details have likely been altered; the stage motif, the "doll" companions, and the pyramid they are building(though Shinji many indeed have seen the Nerv Pyramid in his youth). In this vein, the fact that almost everything else in P3II is a repruposed memory of some kind does make the Shinji/Asuka "sex" scene stand out, and lends some credence to the general theory that the event actually happened. If the others are flashbacks to real memories, why not this? The key point here is that almost everything else is a repurposed memory. Certain things are not. Specifically, the entire "hell-train" sequence, Asuka's tirade followed by her kick, and of course the strangulation scene. Though I do accept the point that we have seen all these other instances in some way before. It's an odd interjection and once which has not yet been adequately explained. However, I'm more inclined to view it as allegorical imagery for what is currently happening rather than going over a new event.
